pr.title: Create separate inner non-fighter HurtCapsule type
pr.author: r-burns

summary: Split fighter hurt capsule storage from the generic lb/it HurtCapsule layout by introducing/using FighterHurtCapsule for Fighter::hurt_capsules and passing its inner .capsule member to generic collision-library routines. The stated motivation was to fix struct size discrepancies between fighter code and lb/item code. The PR also cleaned up item-field accesses that had been abusing xACC_itemHurtbox[1] members as unrelated Vec2/float storage, replacing them with direct xB54/xB5C/xB60/xB64 fields, and adjusted affected prototypes and symbol sizes.
what_changed: Changed Fighter::hurt_capsules from an array of HurtCapsule to an array of FighterHurtCapsule in src/melee/ft/types.h.

Updated fighter collision APIs and local variables to take FighterHurtCapsule* where fighter-specific hurt capsule records are being handled, including ftColl_80076764, ftColl_80078384, and ftColl_8007B5AC.
Updated calls into generic lb collision helpers to pass the embedded generic capsule as &fighter->hurt_capsules[i].capsule rather than the whole fighter-specific wrapper.
Moved direct generic capsule field accesses such as state, skip_update_pos, bone_idx, bone, a_offset, b_offset, a_pos, b_pos, and scale through the .capsule member.
Preserved fighter-only hurt capsule fields such as kind and is_grabbable on FighterHurtCapsule rather than treating them as part of the generic HurtCapsule.
Adjusted item code that previously referenced unrelated data through ip->xACC_itemHurtbox[1].b_pos.z or other hurtbox members to use direct item fields such as xB54, xB5C, xB60, and xB64.
Changed it_80274D04's position output parameter from Vec3* to Vec2*, matching its observed use writing x/y only.
Simplified item helper implementations in it_2725.c by using GET_ITEM, local float temporaries, and direct field names instead of misleading hurtbox-member aliases.
Updated config/GALE01/symbols.txt data object boundaries: it_803F1384 shrank from size 0x94 to 0x44 and three string labels lbl_803F13C8, lbl_803F13DC, and lbl_803F13F4 were added.
smart_moves: Introduced a wrapper-style distinction instead of forcing one HurtCapsule struct to satisfy both fighter and non-fighter layouts, directly addressing the reported struct size discrepancy.
Kept generic lb collision APIs operating on HurtCapsule by passing the inner .capsule, avoiding unnecessary churn in shared library collision routines.
Made fighter-specific APIs explicitly accept FighterHurtCapsule*, which documents when code expects the extended fighter layout.
Replaced misleading item hurtbox aliasing with direct xB54/xB5C/xB60/xB64 accesses, reducing false structural coupling between item data and HurtCapsule.
Changed Vec3* to Vec2* for it_80274D04 where the function only fills x and y, making the prototype better match the data being written.
Updated symbol metadata after correcting data layout boundaries, keeping config evidence aligned with the decompiled types.
decomp_lessons: Do not assume identically named gameplay concepts share one struct layout across systems; fighter hurt capsules can wrap or extend the generic collision capsule used by lb/item code.
When a shared library helper only needs the common prefix/subobject, pass the embedded common member explicitly rather than depending on whole-struct pointer compatibility.
A struct size mismatch is often a sign that local field aliases are wrong, not merely that padding is missing.
Suspicious accesses like xACC_itemHurtbox[1].b_pos.z used as unrelated item state should be treated as evidence of overlapping or misidentified fields and revisited with offsets.
Function prototypes should reflect actual memory usage; if only two coordinates are written, Vec2* may be more accurate than Vec3*.
After correcting data structure boundaries, revisit symbols.txt object sizes and adjacent labels because previously merged objects may split into separate strings or data objects.
naming_conventions: Use FighterHurtCapsule for the fighter-specific wrapper/extended hurt capsule type.
Use HurtCapsule for the generic inner collision capsule consumed by lb collision routines.
Name the embedded generic member capsule and access shared fields through hurt->capsule.field.
Keep fighter-only semantic fields such as kind and is_grabbable directly on FighterHurtCapsule when the diff shows they are not part of the generic capsule.
Use direct offset-style item names like xB54, xB5C, xB60, and xB64 when the semantic type is not yet proven, rather than routing through an unrelated struct member.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Use nested-member access to preserve the same offsets while distinguishing the outer fighter-specific record from the generic collision capsule.
Pass &hurt->capsule or &fp->hurt_capsules[i].capsule into lbColl_* calls to match APIs expecting HurtCapsule* without flattening fighter-only fields into the common struct.
When a decompiled access points into a later element of an array, verify whether the apparent array member is actually a mis-sized struct or unrelated field at the same offset.
For loops over fixed arrays, ARRAY_SIZE(item->x5D4_hitboxes) was used in it_8027146C instead of a hardcoded count of 4, while preserving the same iteration intent.
Local temporaries in it_80274D6C for ECB right/left/top components clarified which floats feed xB5C, xB60, and xB64 and likely helped produce cleaner matching code.
review_feedback: No review comments were present in the provided PR slice.
The PR body/comment evidence only states: "Fixes struct size discrepancies between ft and lb/it code."
follow_up_queries: Find other lbColl_* calls that pass Fighter::hurt_capsules directly instead of the .capsule member.
Audit remaining xACC_itemHurtbox[1] accesses in item code for possible false aliases to xB54/xB5C/xB60/xB64 or nearby fields.
Check whether other systems need wrapper types around generic HitCapsule or HurtCapsule layouts.
Verify that all FighterHurtCapsule-only fields are documented in lb/types.h or ft-related headers with correct offsets.
Review adjacent symbols around it_803F1384 for further object-size splits caused by corrected struct/data boundaries.
